title: Pregnancy Due Date Calculator: EDD by LMP | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate your estimated due date (EDD) in seconds using your last menstrual period (LMP). Get gestational week, trimester, and days remaining with Naegele's…
seoKeywords: due date calculator by due date
pregnancy calendar week by week due date
intro: Calculate your **estimated due date (EDD)** based on the first day of your **last menstrual period (LMP)**. This free pregnancy due date calculator uses **Naegele's rule** — the gold standard in obstetrics — to add 280 days (40 weeks) to your LMP and return your **expected delivery date**, current **gestational week**, **trimester**, and **days remaining** until birth.
keyTakeaway: **Due date = LMP + 280 days** (Naegele's rule, ACOG standard). Only **~5% of babies** are born on their exact due date; a full-term delivery is anywhere between **week 37 and week 42**. An early first-trimester ultrasound (weeks 6–12) is the most accurate way to adjust the EDD if your cycles are irregular.
useCases: Your first question after a positive pregnancy test: "when is my baby due?"
You need the EDD to plan maternity leave and work handover.
You want to know how many weeks pregnant you are today and when each trimester starts.
Planning prenatal appointments: the 20-week anatomy scan, glucose test, dTpa vaccine.
Predicting which week you'll enter the "full-term" window (week 37).

fields.label: First day of your last menstrual period (LMP)
fields.help: The first day bleeding started on your most recent period. Due date is calculated as LMP + 280 days.

dataUpdate.notes: Standard obstetric methodology — used by doctors worldwide.

example.title: Example: LMP was January 1, 2026
steps: **Today** is April 22 → 111 days since LMP = **15 weeks and 6 days** pregnant.
example.result: Estimated due date: **October 8, 2026**. Currently **15 weeks, 6 days pregnant** — 2nd trimester.

explanation:
## ¿Qué es la fecha probable de parto (EDD)?

La fecha probable de parto (EDD, por sus siglas en inglés) es la fecha estimada en que nacerá tu bebé, calculada sumando 280 días (40 semanas) al primer día de tu último período menstrual. Se basa en la regla de Naegele, el estándar de oro en obstetricia. Solo el 5% de los bebés nacen exactamente en la EDD; un parto a término ocurre entre las semanas 37 y 42.

## What is the Estimated Due Date (EDD)?

The **Estimated Due Date (EDD)** — also called **Expected Date of Delivery (EDD)** or **Estimated Date of Confinement (EDC)** — is the date your baby is expected to be born, calculated as **40 weeks (280 days) after the first day of your last menstrual period (LMP)**.

This is called **Naegele's rule**, developed by German obstetrician Franz Karl Naegele in 1812 and still the worldwide standard.

**Formula**: `EDD = LMP + 280 days` (or equivalently: LMP − 3 months + 7 days + 1 year).

## How accurate is a due date calculator?

A calculator based on LMP is accurate **when cycles are regular (28 days)**. Accuracy drops when:

- Cycles are irregular or longer/shorter than 28 days.
- You don't remember the exact first day of your last period.
- Ovulation was late or early.

The **most accurate** way to date a pregnancy is a **first-trimester ultrasound** (weeks 6–12), which measures the crown-rump length (CRL). If the ultrasound EDD differs from the LMP EDD by more than **7 days in the first trimester** (or 10+ days in the second), obstetricians typically **switch to the ultrasound date**.

## Only 5% of babies are born on their exact due date

Terminology matters:

| Category | Weeks | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Preterm** | Before 37 | Requires NICU monitoring |
| **Early term** | 37 0/7 – 38 6/7 | Full-term range begins |
| **Full term** | 39 0/7 – 40 6/7 | Ideal delivery window |
| **Late term** | 41 0/7 – 41 6/7 | Closer monitoring |
| **Post-term** | 42+ | Induction usually recommended |

Only **about 5% of babies** arrive exactly on the EDD. **~80% of deliveries** happen between **week 37 and 41**, most commonly in **week 39**.

## Trimesters (ACOG definition)

- **1st trimester**: weeks 1–13
- **2nd trimester**: weeks 14–27
- **3rd trimester**: weeks 28 onward

## Key prenatal milestones by week

- **Week 6–7**: First heartbeat visible on ultrasound.
- **Week 10–13**: Nuchal translucency scan (Down syndrome screening).
- **Week 20**: Anatomy scan (Level II ultrasound) — detects structural anomalies and usually reveals the baby's sex.
- **Week 24–28**: Glucose challenge test (O'Sullivan) for gestational diabetes.
- **Week 27–28**: Tdap (dTpa) vaccine — whooping cough protection for the baby.
- **Week 35–37**: Group B Strep (GBS) swab.
- **Week 37**: Full-term window opens.
- **Week 40**: Due date.

## Sources & methodology

- [American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG)](https://www.acog.org/)
- [World Health Organization — Maternal health](https://www.who.int/health-topics/maternal-health)
- [Mayo Clinic — Fetal development timeline](https://www.mayoclinic.org/healthy-lifestyle/pregnancy-week-by-week/)
- ACOG Committee Opinion 700: *Methods for Estimating the Due Date* (2017).

## Disclaimer

This calculator provides an **estimate based on self-reported LMP** and should not replace a medical consultation. Always confirm your due date with a healthcare provider and a first-trimester ultrasound.

faq.q: How do I calculate my due date?
faq.a: Add 280 days (40 weeks) to the first day of your last menstrual period. Example: LMP on January 1 → due date October 8 of the same year. This calculator does the math automatically using Naegele's rule.

EDD stands for **Estimated Due Date**. An EDD calculator takes your LMP (last menstrual period) and returns the date your baby is estimated to arrive (LMP + 280 days), plus your current week, trimester, and days remaining.

faq.q: How accurate is the due date from LMP?
faq.a: Very accurate when you know your LMP and your cycles are regular (28 days). Accuracy drops with irregular cycles. A **first-trimester ultrasound** is the most precise way to date a pregnancy and may adjust your EDD by a few days.

faq.q: What if I don't remember my LMP?
faq.a: Ask your doctor for a **dating ultrasound** (best between weeks 6 and 12). It measures the embryo's crown-rump length and produces a reliable EDD even without a known LMP.

faq.q: Will my baby come on the due date?
faq.a: Probably not. Only about **5% of babies** are born exactly on their due date. Normal range is **week 37 to week 42**. Most deliveries cluster around **week 39–40**.

faq.q: What's the difference between EDD, EDC and expected date of delivery?
faq.a: All three are the same thing: the estimated day your baby is expected to be born. **EDD** = Estimated Due Date, **EDC** = Estimated Date of Confinement (older term), and "Expected Date of Delivery" is the plain-English version.

faq.q: Can I use this calculator if I know my conception date instead of LMP?
faq.a: If you know the conception date, add **266 days** (38 weeks) to get the EDD — conception typically occurs about 2 weeks after LMP. Or enter an LMP 14 days earlier than your conception date for the same result in this calculator.

faq.q: When does the third trimester start?
faq.a: The third trimester starts at **week 28** and continues through delivery. It's the final stretch, with more frequent appointments, growth monitoring, and birth preparation.

faq.q: Is this due date calculator free?
faq.a: Yes — 100% free, no signup, no ads interfering with your result. Just enter your LMP and get the EDD instantly.

howToSteps.name: Enter your LMP date
howToSteps.text: Select the first day of your last menstrual period. This is day 1 of week 1.

howToSteps.name: Read your estimated due date
howToSteps.text: The primary result is your EDD — when your baby is expected to be born (LMP + 280 days).

howToSteps.name: Check your current week and trimester
howToSteps.text: The calculator also shows how many weeks pregnant you are today, which trimester, and days until delivery.

howToSteps.name: Confirm with your doctor
howToSteps.text: For final accuracy, have a first-trimester ultrasound — it may refine the EDD by a few days.
